records.ety: [L. sequax, -acis, fr. suquit to follow. See Sue to follow. ]

defs: Inclined to follow a leader; following; attendant.
quotes: Trees uprooted left their place, Sequacious of the lyre.
In the greater bodies the forge was easy, the matter being ductile and sequacious.

defs: Having or observing logical sequence; logically consistent and rigorous; consecutive in development or transition of thought.
quotes: The scheme of pantheistic omniscience so prevalent among the sequacious thinkers of the day.
Milton was not an extensive or discursive thinker, as Shakespeare was; for the motions of his mind were slow, solemn, and sequacious, like those of the planets.
